Glossary About
Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Maurice Merleau-Ponty

1908 – 1961

The body as subject: perception, motility, and the primacy of embodied experience.

Merleau-Ponty’s philosophy of mind is best reconstructed as an anti-Cartesian, anti-reductionist, embodied, perceptual, expressive, social, and finally ontological account of mind. “Mind” is not, for him, an inner substance, an immaterial soul, a private theater of representations, or a set of brain events considered in third-person abstraction. Nor is the body merely the external vehicle of an already constituted intellect. The mind is the meaningful style of a living body’s being-in-the-world: perception, movement, affect, habit, speech, thought, and intersubjectivity are different articulations of one embodied existence.

This does not mean that Merleau-Ponty crudely identifies mind with body. His position is subtler. He rejects both substance dualism and reductive physicalism because both begin from abstractions: “mind” as pure interiority and “body” as objective mechanism. His answer is that the primordial fact is neither mind nor body taken separately, but the lived body, the body-subject, and later the flesh: a field of reversible relations in which sensing and sensed, self and world, body and mind, visible and invisible are differentiated without being absolutely separated. His later ontology tries to radicalize this by showing that even the language of “consciousness” and “subject” still concedes too much to the Cartesian inheritance.

On the question of the soul: Merleau-Ponty does discuss the “union of soul and body,” both in Phenomenology of Perception and in his 1947–48 lectures on Malebranche, Maine de Biran, and Bergson. But he does not defend an incorporeal soul as a separable metaphysical substance. “Soul” names, at most, the psychic, personal, expressive, or spiritual dimension of embodied existence. The union of soul and body is not an interaction between two already separate things; it is “enacted at every instant in the movement of existence.”

1. The philosophical target: two false pictures of mind

Merleau-Ponty’s philosophy of mind is organized around a double critique. On one side stands empiricism, naturalism, mechanism, and classical psychological realism: mind is explained by sensations, stimuli, reflexes, physiological processes, associations, or causal laws. On the other side stands intellectualism, idealism, and transcendental philosophy: mind is explained by judgment, representation, synthesis, or an autonomous constituting consciousness. His claim is that these positions appear opposed but secretly share the same presupposition: they both begin from a world already objectified, a world of determinate things and properties, and then ask how consciousness relates to it.

Against empiricism, he argues that perception is not a mosaic of atomic sensations later joined by association. Perception is already structured: figure and ground, depth, movement, bodily orientation, affective value, and practical significance are not secondary additions to bare sensory data. Against intellectualism, he argues that perception is not judgment imposed upon meaningless data. The perceived thing is not first a neutral manifold and only later an object for thought; it appears from the beginning as a meaningful whole, though one that is indeterminate, perspectival, and open to further exploration.

This is why Merleau-Ponty’s philosophy of mind begins with perception rather than with knowledge, representation, introspection, or neural explanation. The “fundamental philosophical act” is to return to the lived world beneath the objective world: the pre-reflective world in which the body moves, reaches, sees, speaks, desires, avoids, learns, and understands before it explicitly judges.

2. Psychology is not rejected; it is transformed

Merleau-Ponty is sometimes read as if phenomenology simply opposes science. That is misleading. His earliest project was explicitly interdisciplinary. In 1933 he proposed to synthesize experimental psychology, especially Gestalt psychology, with neurology, in order to overcome dominant intellectualist theories of perception. His 1934 research report then adds Husserlian phenomenology as the philosophical framework needed to revise the very notions of consciousness and sensation.

His two major early works, The Structure of Behavior and Phenomenology of Perception, constantly engage empirical psychology, neurology, psychiatry, pathology, child development, Gestalt theory, and psychoanalysis. Schneider, the brain-injured patient studied by Gelb and Goldstein, becomes one of his central cases because pathology reveals structures of normal embodiment that ordinary experience hides. Psychological research is not discarded; it is used as a counterpoint to phenomenological description.

But psychology must be transformed because classical psychology treats the body as an object and the psyche as either another object or a hidden interior domain. Merleau-Ponty argues that psychology cannot simply imitate physics, because the psychologist is also the sort of being being studied. In Phenomenology of Perception, he says that classical psychology failed to grasp the body as a “subject-object,” capable of seeing and suffering, touching and being touched; it therefore reduced the lived body to a representation and treated the union of soul and body as a merely factual Cartesian union.

His Sorbonne lectures on child psychology and pedagogy make this methodological point explicit. He says there is “no difference between psychology and philosophy” in the sense that psychology is an implicit beginning philosophy, while philosophy must remain in contact with facts. Those lectures also show how his psychology is social, developmental, linguistic, and cultural rather than merely individual or neurological.

3. The Structure of Behavior: consciousness as structure, not inner substance

The Structure of Behavior begins with the problem of the relation between consciousness and nature: physical, organic, psychological, and social. Merleau-Ponty’s crucial move is to begin “from below,” with behavior, because behavior is neutral with respect to the classical distinction between the mental and the physiological. Behavior lets us rethink the relation rather than presuppose it.

He rejects both behaviorism and mentalism. Behaviorism reduces conduct to reflexes and conditioned reflexes, but this atomistic account fails even at the level of reflex theory, and still more at higher levels of behavior. Mentalism, by contrast, turns consciousness into an inner cause, a second world, or a force acting on the body. Merleau-Ponty’s alternative is to introduce consciousness not as a thing, cause, or inner reality, but as structure.

The decisive concept is Gestalt or structure. A structure is a meaningful whole that cannot be reduced to the sum of its parts. Organisms do not passively receive stimuli; they organize their milieu according to norms, needs, and possible actions. The nervous system is not a chain of isolated mechanisms but a dynamic field of forces. Behavior, physiology, and perception must be described as forms of organization.

This yields a non-reductive naturalism. Organic life and human consciousness emerge from nature, but nature itself is not the mechanistic aggregate imagined by classical realism. It is structured at multiple levels. Mind is not detached from matter and life; it is a higher-order integration of them. Yet that integration is never absolute, which is why human life remains fragile, ambiguous, and bodily.

This is also where Merleau-Ponty begins to reinterpret the unconscious. He rejects Freud’s energetic and causal metaphors when they suggest hidden forces mechanically producing symptoms, but he preserves the insight that life can have meanings not fully grasped by reflective consciousness. The unconscious is not another personality hidden inside us; it is an “inapperceived signification,” an actual structure of life that organizes experience without being transparently possessed by the subject.

For a philosophy of mind, the result is decisive: consciousness is not a container of mental states. It is a structured way of being oriented toward a world. Meaning is immanent in behavior before it is explicit in judgment. Mind is already worldly, bodily, and practical.

4. Phenomenology of Perception: the body-subject

Phenomenology of Perception gives the mature early account. Its central claim is that the body is not an object owned by a mind; it is the subject’s way of having a world. The body is not merely in space; it inhabits space. It is not a machine moved by consciousness; it is an “I can” before it is an “I think.”

The lived body differs from ordinary objects. I can walk around a table, leave it, return to it, view it from different angles. My body, by contrast, is always with me. It is marginal to all perception, but never merely one perceived object among others. It gives me a point of view on the world before I can explicitly take a point of view toward it. In Merleau-Ponty’s language, the body’s permanence is not just a factual permanence in the world, but a permanence “on my part.”

This is why the body is “ambiguous.” It is visible and seeing, tangible and touching, natural and personal, anonymous and mine. Classical psychology treated such ambiguity as a psychological curiosity, but Merleau-Ponty treats it as ontologically basic. The body is not first a physiological object plus a mental representation; it is the place where the physiological and the psychic are originally intertwined.

Body schema

The body schema is one of Merleau-Ponty’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind. It is not a body image in the sense of a mental picture of the body. It is a pre-reflective, motor, spatial, affective, and practical system by which the body orients itself toward possible tasks. It is the body’s tacit grasp of where it is, what it can do, what counts as near or far, reachable or unreachable, inviting or threatening.

The body schema explains why bodily space is “situational” rather than merely positional. The hand is not given as a coordinate in geometrical space; it is given as a capacity to touch, grasp, point, defend, type, or gesture. The body is not a set of parts side by side; its parts are inter-involved in a practical unity. A Portuguese study by Danilo Veríssimo emphasizes this point well: in Merleau-Ponty, the body schema is “de-substantialized,” no longer a cognitive core inside the subject but an expression of the permeability of body parts with one another and of the body with world and others.

German-language reception makes a similar point through the term Körperschema. Stefan Kristensen describes Merleau-Ponty’s body schema as the body’s “being-toward-the-world,” simultaneously the unity of lived embodiment and the unity of the perceived. He also emphasizes that body schema is a “knowledge without concept” and the ground of practice.

Habit

Habit is central because it shows that mind is not exhausted by explicit cognition. When I learn to type, dance, drive, use a tool, or speak a language, the acquired capacity is not stored merely as a proposition or image. It becomes a bodily power. The body “understands” the movement because it has incorporated a field of possible actions.

This does not mean habit is mechanical. A habit is a flexible style of action responsive to a meaningful situation. The world solicits; the body answers. Patricia Moya’s account in Frontiers in Human Neuroscience captures the point: for Merleau-Ponty there is no hard separation between bodily conduct and intelligent conduct, and habit shows a corporeal knowledge that cannot be explained solely by neurological processes.

Habit also explains tool incorporation. A cane, keyboard, car, musical instrument, or surgical instrument can become an extension of the body schema. Merleau-Ponty says habit expands or “dilates” our being-in-the-world by incorporating new instruments. This is not metaphorical: the tool changes the field of possible perception and action.

Pathology and the intentional arc

Merleau-Ponty often uses pathology to reveal the normal structure of mind. Schneider can perform some habitual or concrete movements but struggles with abstract movements that require projecting himself into a merely possible space. This shows that normal action depends on what Merleau-Ponty calls the intentional arc: the bodily-temporal structure that projects around us our past, future, physical situation, human milieu, ideological situation, and moral situation.

A disorder is therefore not merely a local defect in a mechanism, nor merely a false judgment. It is a disturbance of a person’s way of inhabiting a world. Moya’s discussion notes that pathology can break the nexus between habitual and actual body: the patient may reproduce familiar movements but fail to transpose them into new, meaningful situations.

This is one of Merleau-Ponty’s most important contributions to philosophical psychopathology: mental disturbance is not simply “inside the mind,” and bodily disturbance is not simply “inside the body.” Symptoms express reorganizations of the lived field. Psychic and somatic explanations must often be subordinated to a third level: existence. In Phenomenology of Perception, he explicitly says that the psychophysical event can no longer be conceived as a Cartesian juxtaposition of a process in itself and a cogitatio.

Perception and “qualia”

Merleau-Ponty’s theory of perception is also a rejection of internalist qualia theory. Color, sound, texture, and depth are not private mental atoms. They are qualities of the perceived world as disclosed through embodied orientation. A recent article in Human Studies rightly notes that Merleau-Ponty rejects qualia understood as properties of internal mental states; red and green are not inner sensations but sensibles, qualities of the object as perceived.

This does not make him a naïve realist in the usual sense. Perception is perspectival, embodied, incomplete, and historically sedimented. But its perspectival character is not a defect to be overcome by thought. It is the very way things appear as inexhaustible realities. Perception gives us not private sense-data but the world in profiles, horizons, and possible continuations.

Emotion, affect, and sexuality

Merleau-Ponty rejects the idea that emotions are hidden psychic facts accessible only to introspection. Anger, shame, love, and hate are styles of conduct and forms of bodily expression. This is not behaviorism, because the visible conduct is not a mere external sign of an internal state; it is the emotion’s very mode of existence.

Sexuality, likewise, is not a detachable instinct layered onto a neutral body. It is a way the body opens to others and to the world. It gives the world an atmosphere. Desire is not merely a physiological drive nor a mental representation; it is an embodied intentionality. In this sense, sexuality confirms the broader thesis: the human body is neither a biological machine nor a pure vehicle of consciousness, but a field of existential meaning.

Language and thought

Merleau-Ponty’s philosophy of mind is also a philosophy of expression. Thought is not a fully formed inner content that language later clothes in signs. Speech is a bodily gesture through which thought is achieved, stabilized, and made available. Habitual language can look like a code, but creative speech shows that expression brings meaning into being rather than merely transmitting it.

His Sorbonne lectures on child psychology extend this point developmentally. The child does not acquire language as if deciphering a code with a key; the child lives in an environment that motivates the recapture of language and thought. Merleau-Ponty criticizes Piaget for measuring child language against adult language and missing the positive structure of the child’s communicative life.

For philosophy of mind, this means that thought is not essentially private. Language is not an external instrument of an inner mind; it is one of the ways mind becomes determinate. A mind capable of concepts is already embedded in expressive, social, and historical systems.

Other minds and intercorporeity

Merleau-Ponty’s account of other minds rejects the classic problem according to which I perceive only another body and must infer an invisible mind behind it. I do not first see a physical organism and then reason by analogy to a hidden consciousness. I see anger in the face, grief in the posture, invitation in the gesture, hesitation in the movement. The other is given as an expressive body.

This does not collapse self and other. The other remains other; their experience is not mine. But alterity is not absolute hiddenness. In the Sorbonne lectures, Merleau-Ponty says that “my body and the other’s form a system,” and he connects this to gesture, facial recognition, child development, and embodied comportment.

This is why his account is important for contemporary social cognition. It anticipates what later theorists call embodied, enactive, and direct-perception approaches to intersubjectivity: we understand others primarily through bodily participation in a shared world, not through theoretical inference from external behavior to internal mental states.

5. The soul, incorporeal minds, and the mind-body problem

Merleau-Ponty takes the classical mind-body problem seriously, but he thinks its standard formulation is already distorted. If we begin with two substances, body and soul, extended mechanism and unextended thought, we will never explain how they meet. If we begin with lived existence, however, the problem is transformed. The body is already meaningful, and the psyche is already embodied.

In his discussion of Descartes, Merleau-Ponty notes that the understanding can distinguish soul and body, but the union of soul and body is known by life rather than by pure intellect. Reflection never absorbs the unreflective experience on which it depends; it remains situated in time and place.

The strongest formulation appears in Phenomenology of Perception: the union of soul and body is not an amalgamation of two mutually external terms, subject and object, brought together by arbitrary decree. It is enacted at every instant in existence. In other words, soul-body unity is not a metaphysical bridge between two ontologically complete substances; it is the ongoing event of embodied life.

His lectures later published as The Incarnate Subject show that this question remained central. Those lectures focus on Malebranche, Maine de Biran, and Bergson precisely through the classic problem of the union of body and soul, and they connect the line of reasoning from The Structure of Behavior and Phenomenology of Perception to later developments in Signs and The Visible and the Invisible.

So, does Merleau-Ponty believe in incorporeal minds? As a doctrine of human mind, no. There is no serious room in his philosophy for a Cartesian res cogitans, an immaterial person-substance, or a purely disembodied subject that could have the same perceptual, affective, linguistic, and social life without embodiment. The mind is not in the body as a ghost is in a machine; but neither can it be peeled away from the body. The “soul” is the interiority of a life that is already corporeal.

This remains true even in his later ontology, where he speaks of the invisible. The invisible is not an incorporeal second world. Ideas, meanings, musical phrases, and passions are not reducible to sensible things, but neither can they be detached from sensible appearance and erected into a separate positivity.

6. The later ontology: flesh, chiasm, visible and invisible

Merleau-Ponty’s later work radicalizes the early philosophy. He came to worry that Phenomenology of Perception still retained too much of the language of consciousness, subjectivity, and the pre-reflective cogito. The later project, especially The Visible and the Invisible, attempts to avoid beginning from a subject at all. Instead it begins from the intertwining of perceiver and perceived.

The key term is flesh, la chair. Flesh is not the physical flesh of anatomy. It is also not mind. It is an ontological element: the medium or texture in which sensing and sensed belong together while remaining different. The paradigm is self-touch: my hand touches and is touchable; my body is both sentient and sensible. The relation is reversible, but never perfectly coincident. There is always a gap, divergence, or écart.

The chiasm names this crossing. Body and world, self and other, touching and touched, seeing and seen, speech and being spoken of are neither identical nor externally related. They interweave. The body is not a subject sealed off from the world; it is a sensible-sentient being that belongs to the visible world it opens.

This later ontology changes the philosophy of mind in three ways. First, mind is no longer treated even as embodied consciousness in the old sense; it becomes a fold or articulation of flesh. Second, meaning is no longer merely constituted by a subject; it is latent in the sensible field and becomes explicit through expression. Third, the distinction between perceptual, linguistic, affective, and intellectual life becomes a difference of levels within one ontology of visibility and invisibility.

However, there is an interpretive dispute about this shift. Some interpreters hold that the later ontology simply makes explicit what was already implicit in the earlier psychology of embodiment. Others emphasize that Merleau-Ponty himself criticized his earlier work for remaining partly within a philosophy of consciousness. A recent Human Studies article argues that this self-criticism should not be overstated, because the early works already emphasize the non-conscious and organismic dimension of embodiment; the same article also worries that the later ontology of flesh may blur distinctions between living and non-living beings that The Structure of Behavior preserved more carefully.

The safest reconstruction is this: the early work gives a phenomenology of embodied mind; the later work gives an ontology of the field that makes embodied mind possible. The later work does not abandon the body. It deepens the claim that the body is not an object among objects, by making embodiment exemplary of a more general intertwining of sensible being.

7. The unconscious and psychoanalysis

Merleau-Ponty’s view of the unconscious is neither classical Freudian topography nor simple rejection of Freud. He is attracted to Freud because psychoanalysis undermines the clean separation of bodily life and psychic meaning. Symptoms, dreams, slips, desire, trauma, and affective style show that meaning can operate without reflective possession.

But he resists any interpretation of the unconscious as a hidden mental chamber. In The Structure of Behavior, he replaces energetic causality with structural meaning: the unconscious is an inapperceived signification, an actual structure of lived existence not fully grasped by consciousness.

Recent psychological literature has developed this comparison with Freud by arguing that Merleau-Ponty treats the unconscious not as a boundary between body and mind but as their original coexistence. Conscious and unconscious are better understood through focus and background, visible and invisible, rather than through a hierarchy of inner systems.

This has consequences for psychotherapy and psychopathology. A trauma or fixation is not merely a representation stored somewhere inside the mind. It can be a restriction in one’s lived space, one’s bodily possibilities, one’s relation to others, one’s temporal openness. The unconscious is not behind consciousness like a second mind; it is before us as the structured field in which we move, avoid, repeat, and desire.

8. Extracted philosophy of mind: systematic theses

A comprehensive Merleau-Pontian philosophy of mind can be stated through the following theses.

First, mind is not a substance. It is neither an incorporeal soul nor a brain-object. It is a mode of embodied existence. To ask where the mind is, as if it had to be located either inside the skull or outside nature, is already to accept a misleading ontology.

Second, embodiment is constitutive, not merely causal. The body does not merely influence a pre-existing mind; it is the condition under which perception, agency, affect, language, and selfhood are possible. The body is not an instrument used by consciousness, but the subject’s anchorage in the world.

Third, perception is primary. Theoretical knowledge, science, and explicit judgment are derivative achievements. This does not make them false; it means they arise from a more basic perceptual faith and bodily commerce with the world.

Fourth, intentionality is originally operative and motor. Before “I think that,” there is “I can.” We are directed toward things through possible movements, tasks, and bodily projects. The world is not first represented and then acted upon; it solicits action from within a field of bodily significance.

Fifth, representation is derivative. Merleau-Ponty does not deny that humans use representations, concepts, symbols, and judgments. He denies that these are the basic form of mindedness. Basic perception and action are not mediated by inner pictures or propositions; they are direct embodied engagements with a meaningful world.

Sixth, cognition includes habit and skill. Knowing how to move, speak, play, gesture, or use a tool is not inferior to propositional knowledge. It is a fundamental form of intelligence. Habit is not mechanical repetition but sedimented responsiveness to meaningful situations.

Seventh, consciousness is not transparent to itself. There is no complete self-possession. The subject is temporal, bodily, affective, and historical. We are not Cartesian egos surveying our lives from nowhere; we are situated beings whose meanings often exceed what we can explicitly formulate.

Eighth, the self is neither a pure ego nor a bundle of mental states. It is a temporal style of being-in-the-world, organized by the intentional arc. It includes bodily habits, personal projects, social roles, memories, anticipations, and cultural meanings.

Ninth, emotion is embodied expression. Anger, shame, love, fear, and desire are not private objects hidden inside consciousness. They are ways of inhabiting situations and relating to others. They are visible in conduct, but not reducible to external behavior.

Tenth, other minds are perceived through expressive embodiment. The other is not an inferred inner life behind a neutral body. The other appears as a living, expressive body in a shared world. This gives Merleau-Ponty a powerful alternative to both solipsism and inferential theories of social cognition.

Eleventh, thought is expressive and linguistic. Language does not merely transmit finished inner thought; it helps constitute thought. Speech, writing, art, and gesture are not secondary clothing for ideas but modes through which ideas become determinate and shareable.

Twelfth, the unconscious is field-like rather than container-like. It is the background, latency, or invisible depth of bodily and intersubjective life. It is not a hidden inner theater but the structured opacity of existence itself.

Thirteenth, mind is socially and historically sedimented. The body-subject is not a merely biological organism. It is shaped by language, culture, institutions, tools, social norms, and history. The Sorbonne lectures make especially clear that child development, language acquisition, and intersubjectivity must be understood through social and cultural situation.

Fourteenth, Merleau-Ponty rejects both reductive physicalism and classical vitalism. The organism cannot be reduced to a sum of physical and chemical reactions, but neither does he posit a separate vital force. Life is a norm-governed, meaningful structure irreducible to mechanism.

Fifteenth, the later ontology of flesh gives mind an ontological, not merely psychological, foundation. Mind is not an entity added to matter; it is one articulation of the sensible-invisible field in which bodies, things, others, language, and ideas interweave.

9. Relation to contemporary philosophy of mind and cognitive science

Merleau-Ponty is a major ancestor of embodied and enactive approaches to cognition. Contemporary work by Gallagher, Zahavi, Varela, and others often draws on him because his account of habit, body schema, and perception offers an alternative to both Cartesian introspection and reductive neurocentrism. Moya’s article explicitly connects Merleau-Ponty’s account of habit to embodied mind and cognitive science, while also stressing that phenomenology can correct approaches that reduce bodily knowledge to neurological processing alone.

Against reductive physicalism, Merleau-Ponty would say that a complete third-person description of the body as organism or brain still abstracts from the lived body through which the world appears. Against behaviorism, he would say that behavior is meaningful structure, not a sum of external motions. Against internalist representationalism, he would say that perception is not primarily an inner representation of an outer world. Against dualism, he would say that the mind-body gap is produced by abstracting mind and body from the lived unity of existence.

He is not anti-scientific. Rather, he demands that science understand its own origin in perceptual life. Scientific objectivity is an achievement of embodied subjects in a shared world; it is not a view from nowhere. This is why psychology and phenomenology must mutually correct one another. Psychology supplies pathologies, developmental cases, and empirical descriptions; phenomenology clarifies what those descriptions presuppose about body, world, meaning, and subjectivity.

His account also resists a simple “extended mind” slogan. It is not merely that mind extends beyond the head into tools and environments. More radically, subject and world are not originally two independent things later connected. The body is already a world-involving power; the world is already organized as a field of possible bodily actions. The cane, the instrument, the language, and the other person do not merely add external resources to an internal mind; they reshape the field in which mind exists.

10. Multilingual reception: useful emphases outside the narrow Anglo frame

The English-language reception often emphasizes Merleau-Ponty as a precursor of embodied cognition, enactivism, ecological psychology, or anti-Cartesian philosophy of mind. That is accurate but incomplete. Non-English sources often bring out different emphases.

Portuguese-language scholarship has been especially attentive to body schema and the human sciences. Veríssimo argues that Merleau-Ponty’s body schema is not a substantial cognitive organizer but a figure of permeability among body parts, world, and others. He also stresses that Merleau-Ponty uses medical and psychological research to revise classical antinomies inside psychology itself, especially the subject-object opposition.

Another Portuguese article by Ericson Falabretti emphasizes the concept of structure in Merleau-Ponty’s theory of behavior: Merleau-Ponty refuses both intellectualist and empiricist interpretations of behavior and develops a phenomenological description through the notion of structure, especially in dialogue with Gestalt psychology.

Spanish-language reception often highlights corporeality, flesh, and multidimensional subjectivity. A Chilean article in Cinta de Moebio presents Merleau-Ponty’s body as a way of overcoming disciplinary reductions of the body to technical, biological, psychological, or social fragments. It also interprets flesh as a continuum between tangible and intangible dimensions, not a dualism between two things.

German reception tends to exploit the distinction between Körper and Leib, objective body and lived body. Kristensen’s discussion of Körperschema emphasizes that subjectivity and embodiment are inseparable in Merleau-Ponty, and that the body schema is not merely a psychological concept but a thread leading toward the later ontology of flesh.

Italian work on body schema, such as Francesco Lamberti’s study of Schilder and Merleau-Ponty, stresses that Merleau-Ponty surpasses a merely psychological conception of body schema in order to reach a phenomenological one. This is helpful because it shows that Merleau-Ponty is not simply borrowing empirical psychology; he is transforming its concepts philosophically.

French discussion naturally returns to the classic relation between body and spirit. Pascal Dupond frames the problem of “the body of spirit” as one that can be read dualistically or monistically, but Merleau-Ponty’s own path is better understood as a refusal of both simple alternatives: lived experience shows an intimate link between subjective interiority and material exteriority without reducing one to the other.

Taken together, these non-English discussions reinforce a broad interpretation: Merleau-Ponty is not merely saying that the body causally affects the mind. He is replacing the ontology in which “mind” and “body” first appear as separable terms.

11. Main tensions and unresolved questions

The first major tension is whether Merleau-Ponty ever fully escapes the philosophy of consciousness. His early work still speaks of consciousness, subjectivity, the tacit cogito, and the body-subject. His later work criticizes this vocabulary and turns toward flesh, chiasm, and the visible/invisible. Some interpreters see continuity; others see a genuine break. The strongest reading is a developmental one: he begins with embodied consciousness and moves toward an ontology meant to explain why embodied consciousness is possible.

The second tension concerns science. Merleau-Ponty wants to preserve the truth of naturalism without accepting reductionism. But how exactly should phenomenological description and empirical neuroscience be integrated? Contemporary enactivists and neurophenomenologists have tried to answer this, but Merleau-Ponty himself gives a philosophical orientation more than a research program.

The third tension concerns life and flesh. In The Structure of Behavior, he carefully distinguishes physical, vital, and human orders. In the later ontology of flesh, the continuity between sensing body and sensible world becomes more pronounced. Some interpreters worry that this risks a vague monism of flesh that blurs the difference between living and non-living being.

The fourth tension concerns other minds. Merleau-Ponty rightly rejects the idea that we infer other minds from neutral bodily movements. But direct embodied perception does not eliminate opacity, deception, cultural difference, or misunderstanding. His account works best when combined with his own emphasis on ambiguity: others are directly encountered as expressive beings, but never exhaustively known.

The fifth tension concerns the soul. Merleau-Ponty’s reinterpretation of soul-body union is powerful, but it will not satisfy someone seeking a doctrine of immortality, incorporeal personality, or metaphysical dualism. His position is not a theology of the soul. It is a phenomenological and ontological account of embodied interiority.

12. Final synthesis

A Merleau-Pontian philosophy of mind can be condensed into one formula: mind is the invisible articulation of embodied life in a visible world.

It is embodied because perception, agency, affect, memory, language, and selfhood are rooted in the lived body.

It is ecological because the body exists only as being-in-the-world, oriented toward tasks, solicitations, horizons, and others.

It is enacted because meaning arises in movement, habit, gesture, and practical coping before explicit representation.

It is expressive because thought, emotion, speech, and art do not merely reveal inner states; they bring meanings into existence.

It is social because the body is intercorporeal from the beginning; other minds are encountered in expressive bodies within a shared world.

It is historical because habits, language, institutions, and culture sediment themselves in perception and action.

It is ontological because the later concept of flesh shows that the mind-body relation is one case of a deeper intertwining: sentient and sensible, visible and invisible, self and world.

The soul, on this reconstruction, is not an incorporeal substance. It is the depth, style, and interiority of embodied existence. An “incorporeal mind,” in the sense of a mind separable from body, world, expression, and others, is not a Merleau-Pontian possibility. What survives the rejection of dualism is not a disembodied soul but a richer account of incarnation: the body as the place where nature becomes expression, perception becomes thought, and life becomes meaning.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
